methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series G → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.25x) to $6.0B last post money.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Drones & Autonomous (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 2 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 3 same-sector peers exist in Drones & Autonomous, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: Zipline
private.rationale: Same sector (Drones & Autonomous), same stage (Series G)

data.narrative:
Nuro, a Series G autonomous delivery drone company founded by Dave Ferguson and Jiajun Zhu, currently carries a $6.0B post-money valuation after raising $2.84B to date. The Drones & Autonomous sector comprises four identified private peers—Joby Aviation, Archer Aviation, Elroy Air, and Zipline—with no comparable public companies in the narrow peer set.

Valuation analysis relies on stage-progression methodology, the only applicable triangulation approach given insufficient same-stage or adjacent-stage comparables. This method applies a 1.25x step-up multiplier typical of Series G-to-later-stage transitions, yielding an implied value of $7.5B from the $6.0B base. A peer-median approach was deemed inapplicable: while three same-sector companies exist, none met minimum density thresholds (≥3 datapoints) within same-stage, adjacent-stage, or similar-valuation-tier buckets necessary to avoid misleading broad-sector anchoring.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $6.4B to $8.6B, with a median point of $7.5B. This range reflects the last round as floor and the stage-progression uplift as midpoint. Key uncertainties include the absence of narrow public comparables, limited transaction visibility among early-stage autonomous vehicle peers, and execution risk inherent to commercialization timelines in regulated delivery markets. The single-method foundation underscores reliance on stage dynamics rather than market-based validation.
